sections: History of postal code systems in Germany
first_paragraph: [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") introduced [postal codes](/wiki/Postal_code "Postal code") on 25 July 1941, in the form of a two-digit system that was applied initially for the parcel service and later for all mail deliveries. This system was replaced in 1962 in [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") by a four-digit system; three years later [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") followed with its own four-digit system. Whereas the Federal Republic introduced a system with space left for the East German postal system after a possible reunification, such as by omitting all codes starting with '1' (except 1000 for [West Berlin](/wiki/West_Berlin "West Berlin")) and '9', the German Democratic Republic had a system that used all codes starting from '1' to '9' just for East Germany.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Postal_codes_in_Germany "Special:EditPage/Postal codes in Germany") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Postal codes in Germany"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Postal+codes+in+Germany%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Postal+codes+in+Germany%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Postal+codes+in+Germany%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Postal+codes+in+Germany%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Postal+codes+in+Germany%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Postal+codes+in+Germany%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(November 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") introduced [postal codes](/wiki/Postal_code "Postal code") on 25 July 1941, in the form of a two-digit system that was applied initially for the parcel service and later for all mail deliveries. This system was replaced in 1962 in [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") by a four-digit system; three years later [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") followed with its own four-digit system. Whereas the Federal Republic introduced a system with space left for the East German postal system after a possible reunification, such as by omitting all codes starting with '1' (except 1000 for [West Berlin](/wiki/West_Berlin "West Berlin")) and '9', the German Democratic Republic had a system that used all codes starting from '1' to '9' just for East Germany.

Today, German postal codes are numeric and have consisted of five digits since 1993.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Between 1990 and 1993 the previous four-digit codes in [the former West](/wiki/Old_states_of_Germany "Old states of Germany") were prefixed with the letter "W", and in [the former East](/wiki/New_states_of_Germany "New states of Germany") with the letter "O" (for "Ost", "east" in German). Even though the western system had kept some number ranges free, specifically for later integration of the East in case of reunification, it was decided that the time was right to create an entirely new system for the 1990s, in which larger towns and cities would be divided into multiple postal code areas (the old system had inconsistently used additional numbers after the city's names), and companies receiving large amounts of post (such as mail-order businesses) could be assigned their own private code. This resulted in a system where one could no longer identify the size of the city by the number of trailing zeros in its postal code (such as 2000 for Hamburg or 8000 for Munich).

[Post office boxes](/wiki/Post_office_box "Post office box") are arranged in racks containing several dozens of them. Each rack is identified by an individual postal code.

The 1993 system has geographic zones on the first (Postleitzonen) and on the second level (Postleitregion), e.g., 1 is North East Germany, and 10 is a zone in the inner city of [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/German_postcode_information.png/330px-German_postcode_information.png)](/wiki/File:German_postcode_information.png)

German Postleitzahl map of the first two digits. The green lines mark state borders, which do not always correspond with postal code areas.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Postfachschraenke.jpg/330px-Postfachschraenke.jpg)](/wiki/File:Postfachschraenke.jpg)

P. O. box racks in a German post office of the Duisburg post code area. The top number is the postal code (PLZ=*Postleitzahl*) for the individual rack.

On 31 December 2007, the zones had the following area and population:

| Leitzone | Area (km2) | Population | Region covered | Large cities |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 0 | 37,187.8 | 6,819,607 | [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony"), southern parts of [Saxony-Anhalt](/wiki/Saxony-Anhalt "Saxony-Anhalt") and [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg "Brandenburg"), eastern part of [Thuringia](/wiki/Thuringia "Thuringia") | [Dresden](/wiki/Dresden "Dresden"), [Leipzig](/wiki/Leipzig "Leipzig"), [Halle](/wiki/Halle,_Saxony-Anhalt "Halle, Saxony-Anhalt"), [Chemnitz](/wiki/Chemnitz "Chemnitz"), [Cottbus](/wiki/Cottbus "Cottbus"), [Jena](/wiki/Jena "Jena") |
| 1 | 47,642.4 | 7,034,541 | [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), larger parts of Brandenburg and [Mecklenburg-Vorpommern](/wiki/Mecklenburg-Vorpommern "Mecklenburg-Vorpommern"), small parts of [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony") and Saxony-Anhalt | Berlin, [Potsdam](/wiki/Potsdam "Potsdam"), [Frankfurt (Oder)](/wiki/Frankfurt_(Oder) "Frankfurt (Oder)"), [Rostock](/wiki/Rostock "Rostock"), [Schwerin](/wiki/Schwerin "Schwerin") |
| 2 | 44,207.4 | 8,691,409 | [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg"), [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein"), northern part of Lower Saxony, [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen "Bremen"), small part of Mecklenburg-Vorpommern | Hamburg, [Lübeck](/wiki/L%C3%BCbeck "Lübeck"), [Kiel](/wiki/Kiel "Kiel"), Bremen, [Bremerhaven](/wiki/Bremerhaven "Bremerhaven"), Oldenburg |
| 3 | 45,488.1 | 9,012,212 | southern part of Lower Saxony, eastern part of [Westphalia](/wiki/Westphalia "Westphalia"), northern parts of [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse"), Thuringia and Saxony-Anhalt | [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover "Hanover"), [Bielefeld](/wiki/Bielefeld "Bielefeld"), [Kassel](/wiki/Kassel "Kassel"), [Fulda](/wiki/Fulda "Fulda"), [Gießen](/wiki/Gie%C3%9Fen "Gießen"), [Göttingen](/wiki/G%C3%B6ttingen "Göttingen"), [Brunswick](/wiki/Braunschweig "Braunschweig"), [Magdeburg](/wiki/Magdeburg "Magdeburg") |
| 4 | 20,212.3 | 10,331,535 | north-western part of [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia"), south-western part of Lower Saxony | [Düsseldorf](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorf "Düsseldorf"), [Dortmund](/wiki/Dortmund "Dortmund"), [Essen](/wiki/Essen "Essen"), [Duisburg](/wiki/Duisburg "Duisburg"), [Bochum](/wiki/Bochum "Bochum"), [Wuppertal](/wiki/Wuppertal "Wuppertal"), [Bielefeld](/wiki/Bielefeld "Bielefeld"), [Münster](/wiki/M%C3%BCnster "Münster"), [Mönchengladbach](/wiki/M%C3%B6nchengladbach "Mönchengladbach"), [Gelsenkirchen](/wiki/Gelsenkirchen "Gelsenkirchen"), [Oberhausen](/wiki/Oberhausen "Oberhausen"), [Osnabrück](/wiki/Osnabr%C3%BCck "Osnabrück") |
| 5 | 28,834.5 | 9,233,815 | south-western part of North Rhine-Westphalia, larger part of [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/Rhineland-Palatinate "Rhineland-Palatinate"), small part of Hesse | [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne"), [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn"), [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen"), [Mainz](/wiki/Mainz "Mainz"), [Koblenz](/wiki/Koblenz "Koblenz"), [Trier](/wiki/Trier "Trier") |
| 6 | 17,247.9 | 7,540,503 | southern parts of Hesse, Rhineland-Palatinate, [Saarland](/wiki/Saarland "Saarland"), small parts of [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria") and [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg") | [Frankfurt (Main)](/wiki/Frankfurt_(Main) "Frankfurt (Main)"), [Wiesbaden](/wiki/Wiesbaden "Wiesbaden"), [Darmstadt](/wiki/Darmstadt "Darmstadt"), [Saarbrücken](/wiki/Saarbr%C3%BCcken "Saarbrücken"), [Heidelberg](/wiki/Heidelberg "Heidelberg"), [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim"), [Aschaffenburg](/wiki/Aschaffenburg "Aschaffenburg") |
| 7 | 27,864.2 | 8,715,898 | larger part of Baden-Württemberg, small part of Rhineland-Palatinate | [Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart"), [Karlsruhe](/wiki/Karlsruhe "Karlsruhe"), [Tübingen](/wiki/T%C3%BCbingen "Tübingen"), [Freiburg](/wiki/Freiburg "Freiburg"), [Konstanz](/wiki/Konstanz "Konstanz"), [Baden-Baden](/wiki/Baden-Baden "Baden-Baden") |
| 8 | 36,427.2 | 7,675,001 | southern part of Bavaria, south-eastern part of Baden-Württemberg | [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), [Rosenheim](/wiki/Rosenheim "Rosenheim"), [Augsburg](/wiki/Augsburg "Augsburg"), [Ulm](/wiki/Ulm "Ulm"), [Ingolstadt](/wiki/Ingolstadt "Ingolstadt") |
| 9 | 47,803.7 | 7,163,416 | northern part of Bavaria ([Franconia](/wiki/Franconia "Franconia")), larger part of Thuringia, small part of Baden-Württemberg | [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg"), [Würzburg](/wiki/W%C3%BCrzburg "Würzburg"), [Erfurt](/wiki/Erfurt "Erfurt"), [Weimar](/wiki/Weimar "Weimar"), [Eisenach](/wiki/Eisenach "Eisenach"), [Bamberg](/wiki/Bamberg "Bamberg"), [Bayreuth](/wiki/Bayreuth "Bayreuth") |

There are three states (Saxony, Saarland and Schleswig-Holstein), in addition to the city states (Berlin, Bremen and Hamburg), that lie completely within one postal zone, while three states (Lower Saxony, Saxony-Anhalt and Baden-Württemberg) cover four postal zones.

## History of postal code systems in Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Postal_codes_in_Germany&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History of postal code systems in Germany")]

### Precursors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Postal_codes_in_Germany&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Precursors")]

With the help of ring number stamps, the administration of the [Thurn-und-Taxis-Post](/wiki/Thurn-und-Taxis_Post "Thurn-und-Taxis Post") made it possible for the first time in 1853 to recognize places from a region by a range of numbers. From number 338, assigned on 1 October 1854 to the post office in Lehesten, the numbers are randomly assigned.

In 1917, Carl Bobe divided Germany into metropolitan areas, regional areas and local areas in an organizational scheme.
